diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis of Smith-Magenis syndrome
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Head-banging is a recognised self-injurious behaviour in the syndrome, amplified by sleep loss.
Forceful repeated impact with no pain guarding creates a real risk of intracranial injury.

symptoms.symptom_name: Foreign body (plastic bead) impacted in right ear canal (polyembolokoilamania)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis of Smith-Magenis syndrome
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Compulsive insertion of foreign bodies into orifices (polyembolokoilamania) is characteristic of the syndrome.
A deep, hard, impacted foreign body threatens the eardrum, particularly if manipulated.

symptoms.symptom_name: Prolonged wakefulness >24 hours with inverted circadian rhythm

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis of Smith-Magenis syndrome
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Sleep deprivation is the principal precipitant of behavioural decompensation in the syndrome.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis of Smith-Magenis syndrome
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Self-hugging / spasmodic upper-body squeeze is a hallmark behavioural sign of the syndrome.

symptoms.symptom_name: Reduced pain sensitivity — does not guard or stop when injured

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absent pain protection allows self-injury to continue to a dangerous degree and masks injury severity.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis of Smith-Magenis syndrome
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A hoarse, deep voice is a described feature of the syndrome; here also compounded by prolonged screaming.
Visible trauma confirms the force and repetition of the head-banging.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute otitis media / primary ear pathology driving distress
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No fever makes an infective ear cause of the agitation unlikely; the foreign body is the identified problem.
No otorrhoea/bleeding suggests the eardrum is currently intact, but this must be confirmed on in-person otoscopy.
Absence of hallucinations plus classic syndromic behaviours excludes a primary psychotic disorder.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia and mildly elevated blood pressure (HR 125, BP 140/90)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis of Smith-Magenis syndrome
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Sympathetic response proportional to severe agitation and distress; sealed clinician measurement — the AI Doctor infers distress from behaviour, not a measured vital.

symptoms.symptom_name: Otoscopy: hard bead wedged in the mid external auditory canal, tympanic membrane not visualised

investigation_details.investigation_name: Otoscopic examination of the right ear
investigation_details.expected_result: Hard plastic bead impacted mid-canal occluding the view of the tympanic membrane; left ear and throat clear.
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Depth of impaction and eardrum integrity remain unknown; removal cannot be planned. Cannot be performed via telehealth.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Foreign body in right external auditory canal
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Direct visualisation confirms the foreign body — an in-person examination the AI Doctor cannot perform.

symptom_clusters.cluster_name: Smith-Magenis behavioural phenotype in acute decompensation
symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis of Smith-Magenis syndrome
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Polyembolokoilamania, self-hugging, inverted sleep, reduced pain sensitivity and a hoarse voice together form the syndrome's characteristic acute picture.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person otoscopic examination and foreign-body removal under direct vision
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The impacted bead must be assessed and removed in person; lay removal risks tympanic-membrane perforation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Clinical head-injury assessment (neurological observation +/- imaging as indicated)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Repeated forceful head-banging with absent pain guarding requires in-person assessment for occult intracranial injury.
